summary: Gruber reviews Tweetbot 1.0, Tapbots's new iPhone Twitter client, declaring it his favorite after weeks of beta testing. He highlights two standout interaction design choices: an inline action bar that appears when tapping a tweet (avoiding the typical slide-to-detail-view pattern) and a swipe-right gesture to view conversation threads. He argues these gestures make him feel more efficient than in any other Twitter client on any platform. While acknowledging the Tapbots visual style is sometimes heavy-handed, he praises the obsessive attention to detail, including custom alert animations.
key_points: Tweetbot's inline action bar lets users reply or favorite without leaving the timeline, eliminating unnecessary navigation
The swipe-right-for-conversation gesture is so intuitive it becomes muscle memory that users try in other apps, comparable to Tweetie's pull-to-refresh
Gruber considers Tweetbot more efficient than any Twitter client across all platforms — iPhone, iPad, Mac, or web
Despite finding the Tapbots visual style somewhat heavy-handed, Gruber deeply appreciates the pixel-level attention to detail
money_quotes: It looks good, feels good, and has some clever interaction design.

You know how after you got used to Tweetie's "pull to refresh" feature, you started pulling to refresh in other iPhone apps, even though they didn't support it? That's what Tweetbot's swipe-right-for-conversation feature is like.
I simply feel more efficient in Tweetbot than in any other Twitter client, period — iPhone, iPad, Mac, or web. It's that good.
There's a heavy-handedness to the whole Tapbots style that's a little much for my tastes, but I greatly appreciate the exquisite attention to detail that went into every single pixel of this app.
key_insight: The best interaction design creates gestures so natural they become reflexive habits users expect everywhere, as Tweetbot's swipe-for-conversation did for Twitter timelines.
